advantages: E[X] and Var(X) compress an entire distribution into two interpretable numbers—center and spread—usable for planning, monitoring, and model design.
Linearity of expectation simplifies complex problems via indicator decompositions and additive reasoning without independence assumptions.
Variance (and standard deviation) quantify risk, noise, and instability in metrics, features, and model outputs in consistent units.
Sample mean and sample variance provide unbiased, computationally cheap estimates from data, bridging theory to empirical ML workflows.
disadvantages: Mean and variance alone miss skew, multimodality, and heavy tails—a low-variance distribution can still hide rare catastrophic outcomes.
Variance weights large deviations quadratically, so outliers dominate; MAE and robust statistics sometimes describe typical error better.
Linearity of expectation does not extend to variance or products: E[XY] ≠ E[X]E[Y] unless variables are uncorrelated or independent.
Population moments E[X] and Var(X) must be estimated from finite samples; small n or non-i.i.d. data makes sample summaries noisy or biased.
